Images were first resized and center-cropped before being converted to grayscale for preprocessing.
noise is ill-defined for color images.
Image were reduced in contrast to ensure that adding high-strength noise would not result in pixel-clipping.
To the preprocessed images, we added Gaussian noise of 5 strengths.
The strengths are represented as standard deviations, with values ranging from 0.0 to 0.16.
The spatial-frequency bands are filtered into 7 octave-wide bands, centered at specific frequencies.
The laplacian pyramid consists of seven successive levels, corresponding to the spatial-frequency bands.
There are 29 distinct noise conditions based on strengths and spatial frequencies, with a combination of 4 non-zero strengths and 7 spatial frequencies.
There are 1100 images in the dataset, distributed across different noise conditions and categories.
Images were equally distributed across categories and noise conditions, with approximately 68 images per category.
Sample noise-masked images were used for all 29 conditions as a demo in the experiment.
There is an inverted-U-shaped boundary between images that can and cannot be recognized, peaking at 28-56 cycles/image.
14 human observers performed 16-way recognition of noise-masked images in the experiment.
The human observers went through one training block of 50 trials followed by five test blocks of 210 trials for recognition.
The observers were presented with a blank screen with a central symbol before proceeding with the trials, ensuring fixation at the center.
The randomly sampled image was presented from our dataset.
The image was presented at the center of the screen.
The observer had to identify the category of the image.
The observer had to click on the corresponding button for the image category.

Participants were recruited via Amazon Mechanical Turk.
Participants were recruited for the experiment.

Participants were paid $20 for their efforts.
Each observer signed the IRB-approved consent form.
All observers had normal or corrected-to-normal vision.

The probability of each coarse 16-class ImageNet category was computed as the average of the probabilities of the corresponding fine 1000-class ImageNet categories, establishing a hierarchy relationship between the two.
For each input image, the 16-class category with top-probability was selected as the network’s response, indicating a direct link between the input and output of the network.

Threshold values computed earlier are first mapped to linear indices, establishing a direct relationship between the two concepts.

Bandwidth in octaves and center frequency are calculated using the fitted parameters, establishing a relationship between octaves and frequency.

Cue-conflict images contain shape information from one category and texture information from another. Shape bias is defined as the fraction of images categorized by an observer according to the shape category.
Human shape bias is measured as 0.99, meaning almost 100% of the images are categorized by shape.
The shape bias of all 76 networks is measured using cue-conflict images from a dataset.
Shape bias scores are averaged across object categories to obtain a single number for each network.

Whitebox accuracy is a measure used in analysis to evaluate the neural networks' robustness in the context of adversarial attacks.
